page: Alibaba Cloud ecs.n4.xlarge is an entry-level virtual machine instance featuring 4 vCPUs and 8.0 GB of system memory. Powered by an Intel Xeon 8163 processor operating at 2.5 GHz, the instance utilizes an x86_64 architecture with a shared CPU allocation model. It provides 2.0 GB of memory per core, a 33 MB L3 cache, and runs on the KVM hypervisor. The instance does not include local storage or dedicated GPU hardware, and it features a baseline network bandwidth of 1 Gbps.

Benchmark results indicate that the ecs.n4.xlarge delivers modest performance, with both single-core and multi-core stress-ng CPU metrics falling into the bottom 25%. Memory bandwidth tests show weak read performance at 31.31 GB/sec for large uncached blocks, though write performance for large blocks reaches an average tier at 27.61 GB/sec. Computational tasks such as gzip compression and bzip3 decompression register in the bottom 10%, reflecting the constraints of the shared CPU architecture and older processor generation.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the ecs.n4.xlarge offers a budget-friendly profile suitable for resource-dense environments where raw computational speed is not a priority. The primary tradeoff is the shared CPU allocation, which introduces potential resource contention and limits performance consistency under sustained workloads. The lack of local storage requires reliance on external network-attached storage, which may impact overall I/O performance.

Given its hardware constraints and benchmark profile, this instance is best suited for low-traffic static web serving, basic software compilation, lightweight development or testing environments, and simple background utility tasks. It is not recommended for memory-intensive databases, high-throughput real-time processing, or modern machine learning workloads.
description: Alibaba Cloud ecs.n4.xlarge is an entry-level virtual machine instance configured with 4 vCPUs and 8.0 GB of RAM. Powered by an Intel Xeon 8163 processor running at 2.5 GHz on an x86_64 architecture, this instance utilizes a shared CPU allocation model under the KVM hypervisor. It features a baseline network bandwidth of 1 Gbps and contains no local storage or GPU accelerators. Benchmark data positions the instance in the lower performance tiers, with single-core and multi-core CPU metrics generally falling in the bottom 25%, and memory read bandwidth classified as weak. Consequently, the ecs.n4.xlarge represents a budget-friendly option where resource density is prioritized over raw processing power. It is best suited for low-traffic static web serving, lightweight development environments, and non-critical background tasks.
